name: SovereignSky Projects
description: Open-source tools and resources for digital sovereignty, security, and infrastructure independence
abstract: Open-source tools and resources developed by the SovereignSky community to help organizations achieve digital sovereignty, improve security, and build independent infrastructure.
summary: Explore our collection of open-source projects focused on digital sovereignty, security, and infrastructure independence. These tools are designed to help organizations reduce dependency on foreign-controlled services and maintain control over their digital infrastructure.

itemListElement.name: SecureNet
itemListElement.description: Remote workers are prime targets for state-sponsored attacks. SecureNet ensures that even if a developer's laptop is compromised, attackers cannot pivot into your critical infrastructure - essential protection against foreign cyber operations targeting your organization.
itemListElement.abstract: Secure developer access to internal services without exposing your network

sections.headline: Secure developer access to internal services without exposing your network
sections.description: A 'clean computer within an infected computer' - isolate developer access to internal services through zero trust networking and containers. Malware on the host cannot reach your network.

itemListElement.name: Software Database
itemListElement.description: Every foreign SaaS tool is a potential backdoor for extraterritorial data access. We track what France (SILL), Germany (openCode), Denmark, and other European countries recommend — and aggregate their government-vetted catalogs into one searchable database with sovereign alternatives.
itemListElement.abstract: 4,400+ software products from government-vetted catalogs across Europe, mapped with sovereign alternatives

sections.headline: 4,400+ software products from government-vetted catalogs across Europe, mapped with sovereign alternatives
sections.description: The Software Database aggregates software from multiple government-vetted and expert-curated catalogs into a unified, searchable catalog. Each product is deduplicated, categorized across four taxonomies, and enriched with vendor information, alternatives mappings, and sovereignty assessments.

sections.title: The Problem
sections.description: Organizations want to reduce dependency on foreign-controlled software, but there is no single catalog of European and open-source alternatives. Government-vetted lists exist in France, Germany, and Italy — but they use different formats, categories, and identifiers. Finding what is available, where it comes from, and whether a sovereign alternative exists requires searching multiple sources manually.

left.title: The Challenges
left.description: The same software appears across catalogs with different identifiers. Each catalog uses its own category system. Raw catalog data lacks context. We solve this with 942 product mappings, 10,415+ lines of category mappings, and Wikidata enrichment.

left.title: Goal: Fully Automated Pipeline
left.description: A weekly automated pipeline that scrapes all sources, auto-suggests canonical ID mappings using fuzzy matching, and publishes the updated catalog — flagging conflicts for human review without blocking the pipeline.

items.label: Deduplication
items.description: Ansible appears as ansible, red-hat-ansible, and ansible-automation-platform depending on the source. We maintain a canonical registry with 942 product mappings across 4,400+ raw entries.

items.label: Category Harmonization
items.description: CNCF uses technical hierarchies, SILL France uses publiccode.yml, Euro-Stack uses business focus. We map all to four standardized taxonomies: Technical (43), Developer (26), Business (28), Platforms (8).

items.label: Data Enrichment
items.description: Enrich products using Wikidata for vendor information and web search to validate descriptions and find missing homepages.

itemListElement.name: sovdev-logger
itemListElement.description: Application telemetry often flows to foreign cloud providers, exposing operational patterns and sensitive metadata. sovdev-logger works with self-hosted backends like Grafana and Loki, keeping your observability data under your control and jurisdiction.
itemListElement.abstract: One log call — automatic logs, metrics, traces, and service maps. 95% less instrumentation code.

sections.headline: One log call — automatic logs, metrics, traces, and service maps. 95% less instrumentation code.
sections.description: You write code for yourself during development. But you write logs for the operations engineer staring at a screen at 7 PM on Friday. Good logging is the difference between a 3-hour debugging session and a 5-minute fix.

sections.title: The Problem
sections.description: Traditional observability requires 20+ lines per operation — separate logger calls, metric counters, trace spans, manual correlation.

sections.title: The Solution
sections.description: One call gives you logs, metrics, traces, and correlation — automatically.

code.label: TypeScript — sovdev_log
code.command: sovdev_log(INFO, FUNCTIONNAME, 'Payment processed', PEER_SERVICES.PAYMENT_GATEWAY, input, output);

left.title: No Vendor Lock-in
left.description: Built on OpenTelemetry — Microsoft's recommended standard. Same code works with Azure Monitor in production and Grafana locally.

items.label: Install
items.description: npm install @sovdev/logger

items.label: One line of code
items.description: sovdev_log(INFO, FUNCTIONNAME, "Payment processed", PEER_SERVICES.PAYMENT_GATEWAY, input, output)

items.label: Zero config needed
items.description: Logs to console and ./logs/ directory out of the box. No configuration for local development.

itemListElement.name: DocuWrite
itemListElement.description: Cloud document services scan your content and may share data with foreign authorities. DocuWrite runs entirely locally, ensuring sensitive documentation never leaves your infrastructure - critical for classified, legal, or confidential materials.
itemListElement.abstract: Swiss army knife for converting Markdown to professional PDFs, Word documents, and presentations

sections.headline: Swiss army knife for converting Markdown to professional PDFs, Word documents, and presentations
sections.description: Outdated documentation that stakeholders can't access. DocuWrite creates snapshot PDFs from your Wiki that everyone can share — same version, same meeting, delete after.

sections.title: The Problem
sections.description: Documentation lives in wikis that stakeholders never check. When they need a report for a meeting or a board presentation, someone manually exports pages, reformats them, and hopes the diagrams render. Different people get different versions. After the meeting, outdated PDFs float around forever.

sections.title: The Solution
sections.description: DocuWrite creates snapshot PDFs from your Wiki that everyone can share — same version, same meeting, delete after. Professional formatting with table of contents, numbered pages, searchable text, and Mermaid diagram support. One command, consistent output.

items.label: Professional table of contents
items.description: Auto-generated from document headings.

items.label: Numbered pages with timestamp
items.description: Every page shows generation date for version tracking.

items.label: Fully searchable PDF
items.description: Text-based PDF, not images.

items.label: Auto-generated TODO appendix
items.description: Collects all TODO items into a separate appendix.

items.label: Mermaid diagram support
items.description: Render diagrams from text descriptions.

right.title: Repositories
right.description: Container images for AMD64 and ARM64:

itemListElement.name: Bifrost
itemListElement.description: You cannot secure what you cannot see. Bifrost maps your entire application landscape and data flows, revealing hidden dependencies on foreign services - the essential first step in any sovereignty assessment or migration planning.
itemListElement.abstract: Gain full visibility into your organization's application landscape, integrations, and ownership

sections.headline: Gain full visibility into your organization's application landscape, integrations, and ownership
sections.description: Bifrost provides a structured, authoritative view of the IT landscape — combining technical dependencies with clear accountability and ownership.

sections.title: What sets this CMDB apart
sections.description: Unlike traditional CMDBs that focus primarily on infrastructure, Bifrost treats applications, APIs, integrations, and stakeholders as equal first-class entities — each with unique identities, ownership, and lifecycle management.

items.label: Application Registry
items.description: Each application gets a unique, persistent identifier enriched with business, technical, and operational metadata. Stakeholders mapped via Microsoft Entra ID.

items.label: Integrations & APIs
items.description: Every integration and API is a first-class item with its own identifier, data classification, lifecycle status, and independent stakeholders.

items.label: Dependency Visualization
items.description: Interactive visualizations showing how applications and APIs are interconnected, enabling impact analysis and change management.

items.label: Automation & Integration
items.description: Bulk import from Excel and ServiceNow, auto-generated wikis and PDF catalogs, continuously updated living source of truth.

itemListElement.name: Urbalurba Infrastructure Stack
itemListElement.description: Cloud lock-in is sovereignty lock-in. Urbalurba provides identical infrastructure locally, on-premises, and in any cloud - giving you true portability and the freedom to move workloads to sovereign providers without rewriting applications.
itemListElement.abstract: A complete datacenter environment on your laptop - Kubernetes, databases, AI, and cloud services running locally

sections.headline: A complete datacenter environment on your laptop — Kubernetes, databases, AI, and cloud services running locally
sections.description: Zero-friction developer platform for building, testing, and deploying modern apps without needing to know Kubernetes, GitOps, or the cloud. Same infrastructure locally, in cloud, and on-premises.

sections.title: The Problem
sections.description: Your developers need databases, message queues, AI models, and monitoring — but setting up cloud accounts takes weeks, costs money from day one, and sends your data to foreign providers. Permission bottlenecks, cloud costs, and privacy concerns slow everything down.

sections.title: The Solution
sections.description: 30+ open-source services, pre-configured and ready to run on your laptop, AKS, Raspberry Pi, Ubuntu server, VM, or any Kubernetes cluster. Same stack everywhere. No cloud account needed to get started.

left.title: Runs Anywhere
left.description: Laptop, Raspberry Pi, Ubuntu server, VM, AKS, or any Kubernetes cluster. Same infrastructure in development and production. No cloud account needed — start building immediately, zero cost.

left.title: Key Benefits
left.description: 30+ services, true portability, and data sovereignty:

items.label: Observability Stack
items.description: Complete monitoring with metrics, logs, and distributed tracing. One command.

items.label: Local AI Stack
items.description: Run AI models locally with a unified API and ChatGPT-like chat interface. Your data stays on your machine.

items.label: Analytics Stack
items.description: Collaborative data science with Jupyter notebooks, Spark, and Unity Catalog.

sections.title: Ready to build on sovereign infrastructure?
sections.description: Full documentation at uis.sovereignsky.no

itemListElement.name: DevContainer Toolbox
itemListElement.description: Cloud-based IDEs like GitHub Codespaces send your code through foreign infrastructure. DevContainer Toolbox provides the same convenience locally, keeping your source code and development activity within your own security perimeter.
itemListElement.abstract: A standardized, cross-platform development environment for fast, reliable onboarding and consistent delivery

sections.headline: A standardized, cross-platform development environment for fast, reliable onboarding and consistent delivery
sections.description: A pre-configured development container providing all tools, libraries, and runtime dependencies required to build software—works identically on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

sections.title: The Onboarding Problem
sections.description: New developer joins Monday. Spends the entire first week installing tools, debugging environment issues, and asking colleagues why their build doesn't work. External consultants? Multiply that by the time it takes to get VPN access, security approvals, and the right tool versions. With DevContainer Toolbox, a new developer is productive in minutes. Open the project in VS Code, wait for the container to build, start coding. No setup guide. No OS-specific troubleshooting. No "it works on my machine."

sections.title: How It Works
sections.description: The entire development environment is defined as code and version-controlled alongside your application source. When a developer checks out a repository, they get not only the code, but also the exact tools, runtimes, and configurations required to build, run, debug, and test it.

left.title: AI-Ready Development
left.description: Run AI coding assistants like Claude Code safely inside the container — they can access project files but not your host system. Full AI-powered development without the security risk.

left.title: Works Everywhere
left.description: Identical experience on Windows, macOS, and Linux with:

items.label: New developers
items.description: Productive on day one, not day five.

items.label: External consultants
items.description: Work in a controlled, compliant environment without touching their host machine.

items.label: Teams inheriting code
items.description: Taking over a project from another team or vendor? The devcontainer has the complete environment. No reverse-engineering build steps, no guessing which tool versions they used. Open the project, it just works.

items.label: Team leads
items.description: Scale the team without scaling setup overhead.

items.label: Security teams
items.description: Pinned tool versions, auditable dependencies, nothing installed on host.

itemListElement.name: Dev Templates
itemListElement.description: Starting a new project shouldn't mean days of boilerplate setup. Dev Templates provide production-ready starting points for APIs, web apps, and backends — all pre-configured for the DevContainer Toolbox with consistent best practices.
itemListElement.abstract: Quick, modern, and standardized project templates for rapid prototyping and consistent development

sections.headline: Quick, modern, and standardized project templates for rapid prototyping and consistent development
sections.description: Select a template. Get a complete project with application code, Kubernetes manifests, GitHub Actions workflows, and automatic deployment — all following the same structure.

sections.title: The Problem
sections.description: Starting a new project means days of boilerplate: setting up build tools, configuring Kubernetes manifests, writing CI/CD pipelines, figuring out deployment. Every team does it differently. When someone else inherits the project, they start over.

sections.title: The Solution
sections.description: Pick your template (e.g., typescript-basic-webserver), and you have a running project with local development, CI/CD, and Kubernetes deployment ready to go.

sections.body:
## Available Templates

### Backend Templates

| Template | TypeScript | Python | Java | C# | Go | PHP |
|----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Basic Web Server | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Simple Database (SQLite) | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned |
| Database (PostgreSQL) | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned |
| Message Queue (RabbitMQ/Dapr) | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned |
| Serverless (Knative) | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned |
| Object Storage (MinIO) | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned | planned |

### Application Templates

| Template | React | Designsystemet | Next.js | Storybook |
|----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Basic React App | ✅ | ✅ | | |
| Basic Next.js App | planned | planned | planned | planned |

items.label: Application code
items.description: Working starter app, not just a skeleton.

items.label: Dockerfile
items.description: Container-ready from the start.

items.label: Kubernetes manifests
items.description: Deployment, service, and kustomization for ArgoCD.

items.label: GitHub Actions workflow
items.description: Automatic build and push to GitHub Container Registry.

items.label: Local development
items.description: Run and test inside the devcontainer.

items.label: DevContainer Toolbox (DCT)
items.description: The development environment — all tools pre-configured.

items.label: Dev Templates
items.description: The project starter — you are here.

items.label: Urbalurba Infrastructure Stack (UIS)
items.description: The Kubernetes cluster and services.

items.label: Client Provisioning
items.description: Automated machine setup for managed fleets.

sections.title: Real-World Example: Red Cross Norway
sections.description: The Norwegian Red Cross has 40,000+ volunteers across 380+ local branches. Many are programmers who see how IT can improve daily operations. Dev Templates give these volunteers a structured way to build solutions that central IT can actually adopt — same project structure, same deployment pipeline, predictable handover.

itemListElement.name: Client Provisioning of Rancher Desktop
itemListElement.description: Automated deployment of Rancher Desktop and DevContainer Toolbox to developer machines via Intune (Windows) and Jamf (macOS). A developer gets a new machine, the scripts install everything, and they open any project in a fully configured devcontainer within minutes.
itemListElement.abstract: Automated Rancher Desktop deployment to Windows and Mac machines — via Intune, Jamf, or USB stick.

sections.headline: Automated Rancher Desktop deployment to Windows and Mac machines — via Intune, Jamf, or USB stick
sections.description: A developer gets a new managed machine. The scripts install Rancher Desktop and the DevContainer Toolbox automatically. They open any project in a fully configured devcontainer within minutes.

sections.title: Who This Is For
sections.description: Operations teams in larger organizations responsible for setting up and managing PC/Mac fleets. If your developers use managed machines deployed via Intune (Windows) or Jamf (macOS), these scripts automate the entire developer toolchain rollout.

left.title: Why This Matters for Maintenance
left.description: The biggest benefit isn't just onboarding new developers — it's maintenance. When the maintenance team needs to fix a bug in a system they didn't build, they check out the repo and get the exact same environment the original developer used. Same tools, same versions, same configuration.

left.title: USB Testing
left.description: No Intune? No problem. Scripts can be copied to a USB stick for manual testing on Windows PCs — useful for testing in isolated environments or before MDM is set up.

items.label: Version number and unique ID
items.description: Every script is versioned and identifiable.

items.label: Numbered error codes
items.description: ERR001, ERR002, etc. for consistent troubleshooting.

items.label: --help flag
items.description: Consistent help format across all scripts.

items.label: Structured logging
items.description: log_info, log_error, log_success for CI/CD visibility.

sections.title: Help Wanted: Silent WSL2 Install
sections.description: The biggest unsolved problem: automating WSL2 install on Windows via Intune. WSL2 requires enabling Windows features and a reboot, which is tricky to do silently in an MDM pipeline. If you have experience with Intune and WSL2 deployment, we'd love your help.

itemListElement.name: Refugee ID
itemListElement.description: When 5,000 refugees arrived daily at the Polish-Ukrainian border, there were no IT systems and no way to tell helpers from traffickers. Refugee ID is a QR-based wristband identification system built to protect the most vulnerable.
itemListElement.abstract: QR-based wristband identification for humanitarian operations — built at the Polish-Ukrainian border to stop human trafficking

sections.headline: QR-based wristband identification for humanitarian operations — built at the Polish-Ukrainian border to stop human trafficking
sections.description: Built in partnership with SINTEF during the Ukraine refugee crisis in 2022. Featured in Dagens Næringsliv.

sections.title: The Problem
sections.description: March 2022. 5,000 refugees arrived daily at the Tesco Centre transit camp near the Polish-Ukrainian border. No IT systems. No volunteer screening. No logistics tracking. No big NGOs. No government funding. Just people who showed up. The nightmare: children and women disappearing to human traffickers posing as drivers or aid workers.

sections.title: The Solution
sections.description: With the help of SINTEF, a QR-based wristband identification system was built to distinguish legitimate helpers from traffickers. Every aid worker and volunteer gets a verified QR wristband. Scan it to confirm identity. No wristband, no access to vulnerable people.

left.title: Why It Matters for Sovereignty
left.description: Humanitarian operations handle extremely sensitive personal data — refugee identities, locations, health information. Using foreign-controlled cloud services for this data creates unacceptable risks. These tools demonstrate that critical humanitarian infrastructure can be built on open-source, sovereign foundations.

items.label: QR Armband
items.description: QR-based wristband identification system for aid workers and refugees.

items.label: Wristbands Frontend
items.description: Web interface for the wristband identification system.

items.label: Simple QR
items.description: Python API for QR code generation.

items.label: Safe Monitor
items.description: Safety and communication monitoring system.

sections.title: Helping the helpers
sections.description: This must never happen again — the people who show up must get the tools they need to help people in need.

itemListElement.name: Norwegian Digital Sovereignty Index (NDSI)
itemListElement.description: What gets measured gets managed. NDSI provides a structured framework to assess your organization's sovereignty posture, identify vulnerabilities to foreign control, and track improvement over time - aligned with EU standards and Norwegian regulations.
itemListElement.abstract: A community-driven framework for measuring and improving digital sovereignty readiness in Norwegian organizations

subpages.title: Norwegian Digital Sovereignty Survey
subpages.description: Find out how dependent your organization is on foreign cloud providers
subpages.summary: Answer 11 questions and discover your NDSI level. Get a clear picture of your digital sovereignty risks and concrete recommendations for improvement.
subpages.body:
## How Sovereign Is Your Organization?

Take this 5-minute survey to find out:
- Your **NDSI Level** (0-4) based on the EU Cloud Sovereignty Framework
- **Risk areas** where you're most exposed to foreign dependencies
- **Concrete recommendations** to improve your sovereignty posture

No signup. No data collection. All answers stay in your browser.

---

config.title: NDSI Quick Scan
config.description: Assess your organization's digital sovereignty readiness

elements.name: q1_provider
elements.title: Who provides your organization's primary cloud services (email, documents, collaboration)?

choices.text: US company with EU data centers (Microsoft, Google, AWS)

elements.name: q2_cloudact
elements.title: Are your critical cloud providers subject to the US CLOUD Act or similar foreign laws that could compel data access?

choices.text: No - all providers are EU/EEA companies not subject to foreign access laws
Partially - some services are, but critical data is on EU-only providers
Yes - primary providers (Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, AWS) are US companies

elements.name: q9_contracts
elements.title: Are your cloud service contracts governed by Norwegian or EU law?

choices.text: Mixed - some contracts under foreign law

elements.name: q3_location_core
elements.title: For your core systems (email, documents, CRM, HR, ERP) - do you know where data is stored?

choices.text: No - we don't have full visibility

elements.name: q3b_location_saas
elements.title: Do you have visibility into all SaaS tools used in your organization (Miro, Slack, Notion, Figma, etc.)?

choices.text: Yes - complete inventory, all approved and tracked by IT
Mostly - core tools tracked, but some shadow IT exists
Limited - teams adopt tools without central oversight
No - we don't know what SaaS tools are being used

elements.name: q4_export
elements.title: Can you fully migrate your critical systems (CRM, ERP, collaboration) to another provider - including data, configurations, and integrations?

choices.text: Yes - complete export possible with documented APIs and standard formats
Mostly - data exportable but would lose some configurations/integrations
Limited - can export raw data but system logic/workflows are locked in

elements.name: q10_ownership
elements.title: Do you know the ownership structure of your critical cloud providers?

choices.text: Yes - Norwegian/EU owned, no foreign controlling interests
Yes - EU owned but with some foreign investment
Yes - US/foreign owned (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, etc.)

elements.name: q5_exit
elements.title: Do you have a documented exit strategy for your most critical cloud service?

choices.text: Yes - documented, tested, with timeline and alternative providers identified
Informally - we've thought about it but nothing documented

elements.name: q6_migration
elements.title: How long would it take to migrate away from your primary cloud provider?

elements.name: q7_backup
elements.title: Have you tested backup and recovery procedures for your cloud services in the past 12 months?

choices.text: Yes - full recovery test completed successfully
No - but we have documented procedures

elements.name: q8_alternatives
elements.title: Do you have alternative providers identified for your critical services?

choices.text: Yes - alternatives identified, evaluated, and ready to activate
Yes - alternatives identified but not evaluated
No - but we could find alternatives
No - we're locked in / Don't know

completedHtmlOnCondition.html:
Level 4: Sovereign

Score: {totalScore} / 44

Strong sovereignty posture. You have good control over your digital infrastructure and clear exit strategies.

Recommended Actions:

  • Maintain your current position through regular re-assessment
  • Monitor for changes in provider ownership or terms
  • Share your approach with peers to help others improve

Level 3: Resilient

Score: {totalScore} / 44

Good awareness with some dependencies to address.

Recommended Actions:

  • Document and test exit strategies for critical services
  • Evaluate EU/Norwegian alternatives for highest-risk services
  • Review contracts for jurisdiction and termination clauses

Level 2: Aware

Score: {totalScore} / 44

Significant dependencies identified. You need exit strategies and contingency planning.

Recommended Actions:

  • Map all cloud dependencies and their jurisdictions
  • Create exit strategy for your most critical service
  • Test backup recovery procedures

Level 1: Dependent

Score: {totalScore} / 44

High dependency on foreign providers with limited resilience. Priority action needed.

Recommended Actions:

  • Identify where your critical data is actually stored
  • Understand CLOUD Act implications for your organization
  • Start contingency planning immediately

Level 0: Unassessed

Score: {totalScore} / 44

Critical gaps identified. Many 'Don't know' answers indicate areas needing immediate investigation.

Recommended Actions:

  • Map all cloud services your organization uses
  • Identify the legal jurisdiction of each provider
  • Determine where your data is physically stored

sections.headline: A community-driven framework for measuring and improving digital sovereignty readiness in Norwegian organizations
sections.description: The Norwegian Digital Sovereignty Index is a practical framework for measuring how dependent your organization is on foreign-controlled digital infrastructure. Based on the EU Cloud Sovereignty Framework.

sections.title: The Problem
sections.description: Organizations know they depend on foreign cloud providers, but they have no standardized way to measure how dependent they are, compare themselves to peers, or track improvement over time.

items.label: NDSI Quick Scan Survey
items.description: 11 questions covering provider jurisdiction, data control, exit readiness, and operational resilience.

items.label: Software Risk Check
items.description: Select the tools your organization uses and get an instant risk assessment based on jurisdiction, data location, and portability.

items.label: Software Database
items.description: Risk scores for individual software products, mapping to European alternatives, vendor ownership data.

right.title: Current Status
right.description: The framework is in planning phase. The survey and basic scoring are functional, but full integration with the Software Database for automated risk assessment is under development.
